content.subtitle: Use
items: Singular countable nouns (when not being specific or when mentioning something for the first time): There's a good film on TV tonight.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: Whether we use 'an' or 'a' depends on the sound, not the spelling.

examples.correct: The news is on TV in **an** hour.

examples.incorrect: The news is on TV in **a** hour.

examples.correct: Being in a film was **a** unique experience for me.

examples.incorrect: Being in a film was **an** unique experience for me.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Singular countable nouns (when being specific): Where's the DVD you were talking about?
Singular countable nouns (when talking generally): The radio seemed amazing to people at first.
Plural countable nouns (when being specific): I didn't believe the rumours about the prime minister.
Uncountable nouns (when being specific): I followed the advice my lawyer gave me.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: The way we say 'the' changes depending on the sound at the start of the next word. Before a consonant sound (*the media*, *the USA*, *the programme*), we pronounce it /ðə/. Before a vowel sound (*the announcer*, *the actor*, *the editor*), we pronounce it /ði/.
We often use 'the' when we are talking about something there is only one of.
✓ **the** sky, **the** sun, **the** moon, **the** Prince of Wales, **the** North Pole, **the** World Cup

content.description: We often don't use an article at all. This is sometimes called the zero article.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Plural countable nouns (when talking generally): Journalists often face dangerous situations.
Uncountable nouns (when talking generally): News travels fast these days.

content.description: Notice how we use articles in the following phrases and expressions. Many of these are examples of general categories. For example, '**the** River Thames' means that we use 'the' for all rivers.

rows.label: Time
items: definite article: in the 1990s, in (the) summer, in the morning
zero article: in 2008, in June, on Friday, at night

rows.label: People and work
items: indefinite article: have a job, work as a ...
definite article: the queen, the principal, the president, the French
zero article: become president, go to work, be at work, have work to do, he's French

rows.label: Places
items: definite article: the Alps, the Atlantic Ocean, the River Thames, the Earth, the Arctic, the USA, the UK, the Cyclades
zero article: Mount Everest, Paris, America, Mars/Venus/Jupiter etc, Oxford Street, Lake Superior, Crete

rows.label: Entertainment and sport
items: definite article: the media, on the radio, play the guitar, go to the cinema, watch (the) TV
zero article: listen to music, on television, play tennis/football etc

rows.label: Organisations
items: definite article: the army, the police, the fire brigade

rows.label: Education
items: definite article: go to the school (as a visitor), be in the first year
zero article: go to school (as a student), be in Class 3B, maths

rows.label: Travel
items: indefinite article: take a taxi, catch a/the bus
definite article: on the bus
zero article: on foot, go home, go by bus

rows.label: Health
items: indefinite article: have a cold/headache/cough
definite article: have (the) flu/measles
zero article: have toothache

rows.label: Public buildings
items: definite article: the bank, the tax office, go to the hospital/prison (as a visitor)
zero article: go to hospital/prison/church (as a patient/prisoner/worshipper)

content.subtitle: American English note
content.description: US speakers use 'the hospital' where UK speakers say 'to hospital'.
examples: US: The ambulance took Simon to the hospital. UK: The ambulance took Simon to hospital.

useItems: Use a singular or plural verb
examples: There are **some** good **articles** in the paper.
How **much information** do we have about it?

fullWidthRows: Common uncountable nouns: advice, coffee, furniture, glass, hair, homework, information, knowledge, luggage, money, news, paper, work

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Most uncountable nouns are singular, but a few are plural. These include *clothes, scissors, jeans, spectacles, trousers, groceries*, etc. With these words, we use a plural verb.
✓ Oh, no! My new clothes **are** dirty!
Some nouns are countable with one meaning and uncountable with another meaning.
✓ Do you think you could bring me **a** clean **glass**? (countable)
✓ We should make computer monitors out of recycled **glass**. (uncountable)

rows.use: countable nouns, usually in negative statements and questions
rows.example: There aren't **many** programmes on TV that I find interesting.

rows.use: uncountable nouns, usually in negative statements and questions
rows.example: My dad never shows **much** interest in the news.

rows.use: countable and uncountable nouns in positive statements
rows.example:
That film has won **a lot of / lots of** awards.
What **a lot of** luggage you've got!
There have been **a few** scandals in the papers recently.
They say that **a little** knowledge is a dangerous thing.
The police have **little** information about the robbery.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: The phrase '**only a few**' means 'not many'. The phrase '**only a little**' means 'not much'.
✓ There are **only a few** programmes on TV that I like watching.
✓ There's **only a little** sugar left, so get some when you go out.

sections.title: A: Articles
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I love this time in **evening / the evening** when the sun is going down.
People play **cricket / the cricket** in South Africa, Australia and Sri Lanka.
It's too far to walk so I think I'll catch **bus / the bus**.
Jane had to go home from school because she had **a headache / the headache**.
The robber was sent to **prison / the prison** for a total of three years.
My cousin works as **waiter / a waiter** in a cafe near where we live.
The underground doesn't run this late so we'll have to take **a taxi / the taxi**.
I like to listen to **music / the music** in my free time.
My favourite subject at school is **chemistry / the chemistry**.
Jazz music appeared in America in **1920s / in the 1920s**.

sections.title: B: Multiple choice (Articles)
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer. If no word is needed, choose 'D'.

items.sentence: Oh, I didn't tell you! We've got ...... new English teacher.
Here's ...... DVD you asked to borrow.
We're out of ...... coffee, so could you get some from the supermarket?
The prize is ...... unique opportunity to travel the world!
It looks like ...... glass in your bedroom window is cracked.
It's ...... honour to be here this evening to speak to you.
There's ...... good chance we'll be late for the meeting.
Reports are coming in of a major oil spill in ...... Mediterranean.
I went to see the doctor because I'm finding it difficult to sleep at ...... night.
Do you think that they'll ever send a manned mission to ...... Venus?

sections.title: C: Error correction (extra words - articles)
sections.instructions: Circle the extra word in each line. Click it.

items.sentence: [The] many people who appear in the news for the first time
find that [a] sudden fame has a negative effect on their lives.
The national press can be an unforgiving and [the] loss of
the privacy that comes with fame is an extra problem at a
difficult time. This is especially the case when a someone is
in the news because of a tragedy or [the] serious crime.
Just at the moment when you are under a most stress, you find
microphones and the cameras in your face.
The media have a responsibility to inform the public, but an innocent people
are often hurt by the demand for [the] scandal.

sections.title: D: Countable/uncountable (verb agreement)
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: The clothes you bought me for my birthday ...... (be) wonderful!
It turned out that the information we had received ...... (be) wrong.
My new jeans ...... (be) in the wash and I wanted to wear them tonight.
The luggage on a plane ...... (take) up a lot of room.
Your hair ...... (look) really nice and shiny.
I could tell by the look on her face that the news ...... (not / be) good.
Oh, no! My money ...... (be) in my other jacket!

sections.title: E: Error correction (countable/uncountable)
sections.instructions: Find ten mistakes in the passage and type the corrections in the gaps next to each bold error.
sections.passage: We interrupt your usual schedule to bring you **an important news** (1) ...... We are receiving **informations** (2) ...... about a spaceship — it has landed in Central Park, New York! The large ship seems to be made of **glasses** (3) ...... Eye-witnesses described them as short and said their clothes **was** (4) ...... made of metal, and their **hairs** (5) ...... was bright green. Strangely, one of them appeared to be wearing **a jean** (6) ...... **Much** (7) ...... people said that they seemed to be friendly. Scientists believe we can exchange **knowledges** (8) ...... with these visitors. Police advice **are** (9) ...... to stay indoors for the time being. We will be back with **another news** (10) ...... as soon as we can.

sections.title: F: Key Word Transformations (Quantifiers)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Not many people came to the party.
**only** There ...... people at the party.

The witness described the mugger to the police.
**a** The witness ...... of the mugger.

There isn't much sugar left.
**a** There ...... sugar left.

I am thinking of becoming a firefighter when I leave school.
**brigade** I am thinking of ...... when I leave school.

We were shocked by the news and didn't know what to do.
**shocking** The news ...... didn't know what to do.

There aren't many good films out at the moment.
**only** There ...... good films out at the moment.

I didn't eat much dessert because I'm on a diet.
**only** I ...... dessert because I'm on a diet.

You have lots of hats!
**lot** What ...... you have!
I'm quite busy so I've got **H** very little time to talk to you now.
Happily, we have had **F** very few complaints from hotel guests recently.
My dad just got a good job so we have **G** a little money for luxuries.
Pollution is improving now that **B** a few people recycle their rubbish.
My mum has lost her job so we have **D** little money for luxuries.
My lesson's been cancelled so I've got **A** a little time to talk to you now.
It's a shame that **E** few people recycle their rubbish.
Unfortunately, we have had **C** a few complaints from hotel guests recently.
If you have ...... time at the end of the exam, check your answers.
Give me ...... minutes and I'll be ready.
We've only got ...... milk left so get some when you go shopping.
Just ...... practice every day and you'll soon be able to play the piano.
We were disappointed that ...... of the members came to the youth club party.
Sprinkle ...... sugar on the strawberries.
I suppose now I'm 43, I have ...... hope of playing football for England.
Could you help me with ...... exercises I don't understand?
There's ...... coffee left, if anyone wants some.
Becoming an astronaut is so demanding that ...... people manage it.
I'll meet you in half an hour - I've got ...... e-mails to write first.
Many people feel there's ...... point in voting, even in a general election.

sections.title: I: Cloze (Articles)
sections.instructions: Write an article in each gap where necessary. If an article is not necessary, write a dash (-).

items.sentence: Ray: Hello? Sandy: Hi. Is that Ray? It's (1) ...... Sandy.
Ray: Oh, hi! How was (2) ...... film?
Sandy: Great! We took (3) ...... taxi to (4) ...... cinema and (5) ...... taxi driver got lost...
...so we almost missed (6) ...... start.
What about you? What did you do (7) ...... last night?
Ray: Oh, I just stayed at (8) ...... home. I listened to (9) ...... music for a while and then watched (10) ...... news.
Sandy: Oh, yes? So what's happening in (11) ...... world?
Ray: Well, I know you don't like (12) ...... politics, but there was (13) ...... interesting report on (14) ...... Asia.
It gave me (15) ...... few ideas for (16) ...... geography essay we were talking about (17) ...... other day.
Ray: Nothing important. Just that (18) ...... lead singer of your favourite band is getting married - to (19) ...... actress, (20) ...... one who was in that film ...
Sandy: What?! Why didn't you tell me? I have to check this out on (21) ...... Internet! See you at (22) ...... school tomorrow. Bye!

sections.title: J: Error correction (Articles)
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences correctly, adding articles where necessary.

items.context: We went to theatre last night and saw great play, although some of acting was bit poor.
items.answer: We went to the theatre last night and saw a great play, although some of the acting was a bit poor.

items.context: Give me ring tomorrow before I go to work and we'll arrange to meet at office.
items.answer: Give me a ring tomorrow before I go to work and we'll arrange to meet at the office.

items.context: Prime minister told reporter that government wanted to pass law banning hunting.
items.answer: The Prime Minister told the reporter that the government wanted to pass a law banning hunting.

items.context: I asked hotel manager where pool was and she directed me to seventh floor.
items.answer: I asked the hotel manager where the pool was and she directed me to the seventh floor.

items.context: They said on news on radio that Mount Vesuvius, volcano that destroyed Pompeii, could erupt again.
items.answer: They said on the news on the radio that Mount Vesuvius, the volcano that destroyed Pompeii, could erupt again.

items.context: Car has revolutionised transport and is absolutely vital to economy of most countries.
items.answer: The car has revolutionised transport and is absolutely vital to the economy of most countries.

items.context: Judge read jury's verdict aloud and then sentenced accused to five years in prison.
items.answer: The judge read the jury's verdict aloud and then sentenced the accused to five years in prison.

items.context: Job in media can be quite stressful because of pressure you are under to do things on time.
items.answer: A job in the media can be quite stressful because of the pressure you are under to do things on time.

items.context: Very few people in 19th century went to school or university and most started work at very young age.
items.answer: Very few people in the 19th century went to school or university and most started work at a very young age.

items.context: I have little free time since I gave up karate, so I'm thinking of trying new hobby, like learning musical instrument.
items.answer: I have little free time since I gave up karate, so I'm thinking of trying a new hobby, like learning a musical instrument.
